coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole to l-cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Wenfang
Cheng, Chang
Yang, Hui
Wang, Hui
Tan, Yueming
Xie, Qingji
Ma, Ming
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: We report here a sensitive method for immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD-Cl) to l-cysteine (Cys). S. aureus cells were separated from samples by immunomagnetic nanoparticles and labeled with Cys-encapsulated liposome (Cys@liposome) immuno-nanocapsules. After mangnetic separation, the Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules linked to bacterial cells were destructed by Tween-20, and then the released Cys molecules reacted with NBD-Cl to generate colorimetric and fluorescent signals. Due to the amplification effect of liposome nanocapsules, the high capture efficiency of immunomagnetic nanoparticles, and the high responses of NBD-Cl to Cys, the detection limits for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of S. aureus were as low as 10 and 1 CFU mL−1, respectively. The proposed method was successfully used to detect S. aureus in milk samples. This work has provided a novel NBD-Cl based colorimetric and fluorescent platform for immunoassay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay
4-Chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole
l-cysteine
Pathogenic bacteria
Staphylococcus aureus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules
Colorimetric and fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to Cys
Preparation and characterizations of GLCNS Cys@liposome nanocapsules
Colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay of S. aureus

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay of S. aureus.

(a) UV–vis spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl before and after reaction with 20 μM Cys for 15 min in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3). Black curve shows UV–vis spectrum of 20 μM Cys aqueous solution. (b) Fluorescence spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl after reaction with 20 μM Cys for 15 min in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3).

(a, b) Bright field TEM image (a) and dark filed TEM image (b) of Cys@liposome nanocapsules. (c) FTIR spectra of Cys, liposome, and Cys@liposome nanocapsules. (d) Fluorescence spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl after reaction with the undestructed Cys@liposome nanocapsules and the destructed Cys@liposome nanocapsules in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3).

Photographs (a), UV–vis absorption spectra (b), absorbance at 475 nm (c), fluorescence spectra (d), and fluorescent intensity at 555 nm of the resulting solutions for the detection of different concentrations of S. aureus cells. Inset of Fig. 4c and e show UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra for the detection of ultralow concentrations of S. aureus cells, respectively.

Absorbance at 475 nm (a) and fluorescence intensities at 555 nm (b) for the detection of S. aureus, S. spp., E. coli O157:H7, and L. monocytogenes (2000 CFU mL−1 for S. aureus, 20000 CFU mL−1 for other pathogens).
The results for the determination of S. aureus in milk samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole to l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education of China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report here a sensitive method for immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD-Cl) to l-cysteine (Cys). S. aureus cells were separated from samples by immunomagnetic nanoparticles and labeled with Cys-encapsulated liposome (Cys@liposome) immuno-nanocapsules. After mangnetic separation, the Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules linked to bacterial cells were destructed by Tween-20, and then the released Cys molecules reacted with NBD-Cl to generate colorimetric and fluorescent signals. Due to the amplification effect of liposome nanocapsules, the high capture efficiency of immunomagnetic nanoparticles, and the high responses of NBD-Cl to Cys, the detection limits for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of S. aureus were as low as 10 and 1 CFU mL−1, respectively. The proposed method was successfully used to detect S. aureus in milk samples. This work has provided a novel NBD-Cl based colorimetric and fluorescent platform for immunoassay.

Ultrasensitive immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus is described based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to l-cysteine.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogenic bacteria are one of the most important factors affecting food safety, so the detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria is important. Culture-based methods are considered to be the gold standard for the detection pathogenic bacteria, but they have the shortcoming of long detection cycle [1]. Some modern detection technologies, such as polymerase chain reaction technology and immunology-based enzyme linked immunosorbent assay, have been used for the detection of pathogenic bacteria [2,3]. However, these detection technologies still possess some disadvantages, such as complicated detection procedures, high cost, and high technical requirements. Recently, colorimetric and fluorescent detection of pathogenic bacteria has been widely reported by using biological recognition agents to specifically recognize and capture target pathogenic bacteria, showing the advantages of low cost, fast response, and simple operation [4–11]. However, the detection limits of these methods are typically higher than 102 CFU mL−1 [4–11], which are still difficult to meet the requirements of practical applications, since the infectious doses of many pathogenic bacteria are as low as 10 cells. Therefore, it is important to develop a new method for rapid and sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria.

Herein, a sensitive method for immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) was developed based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to Cys. Cys encapsulated liposome (Cys@liposome) immuno-nanocapsules were prepared and used as novel labels for the detection of S. aureus. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules and immunomagnetic nanoparticles specifically recognized S. aureus cells in samples. After mangnetic separation and liposome destruction, the Cys molecules released from the immuno-nanocapsules were used for colorimetric and fluorescent analysis with NBD-Cl as the probe. Due to the amplification effect of liposome nanocapsules, the high capture efficiency of immunomagnetic nanoparticles, and the high responses of NBD-Cl to Cys, the detection limits were as low as 10 and 1 CFU mL−1 for colorimetric and fluorescent detection, respectively. We believe that the NBD-Cl based platform can be extended to immunoassay of many other analytes by simply choosing the biological recognition agents.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies were performed on a TECNAI F-30 high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) studies were performed on a Hitachi S4800 scanning electron microscope. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer (Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected on Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer.

NBD-Cl and amino functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (diameter 100–200 nm) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Cys and Tween-20 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine, dipalmitoyl phosphocholine, cholesterol, N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), E. coli O157:H7, Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), and Salmonella spp. (S. spp.) cells were obtained from American Type Culture Collection. The anti-S. aureus antibody (molecular weight: 48 kDa) was purchased from Abcam. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.0) consisted of 0.10 M KH2PO4–Na2HPO4. All other chemicals were of analytical grade or better quality. Millipore Milli-Q water (18 MΩ cm−1) was used in all experiments.

Dipalmitoyl phosphocholine (30 μmol), cholesterol (30 μmol), and dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine (5 μmol) were dissolved in 4 mL of chloroform, followed by the addition of 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 7.0) containing 10 mM Cys. After sonication for 5 min, the organic solvent was removed by rotary evaporation under reduced pressure at 40 °C to form a thin lipid film. Then the film was hydrated in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS for 1 h with vigorous shaking at 40 °C, and then sonicated for 10 min. Finally, the liposome fraction was dialyzed to remove the unencapsulated reagents using a membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 (MD34-3500) against 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) for 24 h at 4 °C, and stored in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules
ce:section.ce:para: EDC·HCl (4 mg) and NHS (2 mg) were added in 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 1 mg mL−1 Cys@liposome nanocapsules, followed by the addition of 100 μL of 0.1 mg mL−1 anti- S. aureus antibody. After vigorous shaking for 12 h at room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with 0.1 M PBS to remove the excess reagents. The final product was blocked with 1 mL of PBS containing 1 wt% BSA at room temperature with gentle agitation for 30 min, then centrifuged, dispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0), and stored at 4 °C for future use. Immunomagnetic nanoparticles were prepared similarly to Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules except that amino-functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles were used instead of Cys@liposome nanocapsules.

Sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) was carried out to confirm the successful modification of the anti-S. aureus antibody on the surfaces of various samples. In the experiment, 2 μL of sample was mixed with 8 μL of Milli-Q water and 2 μL of 6 × loading buffer, and then the mixture was loaded into SDS-PAGE gel. A 0.5 M Tris/boric acid buffer was used as the running buffer. Finally, the SDS-PAGE gels were run at a voltage of 120 V for 1 h.

S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 grew in sterile Luria-Bertani (LB) media (2.0 g bacto-tryptone, 1.0 g bacto-yeast extract powder, 2.0 g NaCl dissolving in 200 mL distilled water, and adjusted to pH 7.4 with 3.0 M NaOH) in an incubator-shaker at 37 °C to reach the growing stationary phase. After overnight incubation, 1 mL of bacterial cells was centrifuged (6000 rpm, 10 min) to remove the supernatant and washed 3 times with PBS, and resuspended in 1 mL of PBS. Howbeit, L. monocytogenes and S. spp. grew in Nutrient-Broth (NB) media (2.0 g bacto-peptone, 0.6 g bacto-beef extract, 1.0 g NaCl dissolving in 200 mL distilled water, and adjusted to pH 7.4 with 3.0 M NaOH). By plating bacteria on LB or NB plates, the amount of bacteria per milliliter can be acquired by counting related colony forming units (CFU) after incubation overnight at 37 °C.

100 μL of 0.1 M PBS containing 1 mg mL−1 Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules was first mixed with 100 μL of 0.1 M PBS containing 0.4 mg mL−1 immunomagnetic nanoparticles, and the mixture was incubated with 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS containing different concentrations of S. aureus cells for 60 min at room temperature. Then magnetic separation was carried out, and the product collected by a controlled magnetic field was dispersed in 1 mL 0.1 M PBS. Then, 10 μL of Tween-20 was added into the supernatant solution and maintained for 30 min to release Cys molecules from the Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules. Finally, immunomagnetic nanoparticles were removed by magnetic field, and 20 μL of the above solution was mixed with 20 μL of CH3CN solution containing 10−3 M NBD-Cl, followed by the addition of 960 μL PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3). The as-obtained solution was used for colorimetric and fluorescent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric response of NBD-Cl to Cys was studied. The reaction between NBD-Cl and Cys was carried out in a mixed solution composed of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) and CH3CN. In the UV–vis spectrum of Cys (Fig. 2 a), no absorption band between 300 and 700 nm is observed. NBD-Cl shows an absorption peak at 340 nm. After reaction with Cys for 15 min in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3) at room temperature, the peak at 340 nm for NBD-Cl decreases, and a new absorption peak at 475 nm can be observed, due to the replacement of -Cl group by Cys (Scheme S1) [12–14]. To achieve the highest colorimetric response of NBD-Cl to Cys, the reaction media was optimized. The optimal volume ratio between PBS and CH3CN is 7:3 (Fig. S1a), and the optimal solution pH is 7.0 (Fig. S1b). Fig. S2a shows photographs of 20 μM NBD-Cl after reaction with different concentrations of Cys for 15 min in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3). With the increase of Cys concentration, the color of the solutions gradually deepens, due to the formation of yellow NBD-Cys [12–14]. Especially, the generating chromogenic signals of the solutions in Cys concentrations from 0 to 10 μM can be distinguished by naked eyes. Fig. S2b shows UV–vis spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl after reaction with different concentrations of Cys for 15 min in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3). The intensity of the absorption peak at 340 nm gradually decreases, while the intensity of the absorption peak at 475 nm gradually increases. As shown in Fig. S2c, the absorbance at 475 nm linearly increases with the concentrations of Cys. The linear range is from 0.1 to 20 μM (R 2 = 0.9972), with a detection limit of 50 nM.

The fluorescent response of NBD-Cl to Cys was also studied. NBD-Cl is a nonfluorescent, because the two electron-withdrawing groups (-Cl and –NO2) can result in a pull-pull system and block intramolecular charge transfer process [12–14]. As shown in Fig. 2b, after reaction with 20 μM Cys for 15 min in PBS-CH3CN solution, the reaction product, NBD-Cys becomes highly fluorescent, because the replacement of -Cl group by Cys can result in a pull-push system and enable intramolecular charge transfer [12–14]. The fluorescent spectra in Fig. 2b show that NBD-Cys have an optimal excitation wavelength at 475 nm and an optimal emission wavelength at 555 nm. Fig. S3a shows fluorescence spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl at an excitation wavelength of 475 nm after reaction with different concentrations of Cys for 15 min. As shown in Fig. S3b, the fluorescent intensity at 555 nm is proportional to the concentrations of Cys. The linear range is from 0.02 to 20 μM (R 2 = 0.9955), with a detection limit of 5 nM.

The kinetics of the reaction between NBD-Cl and Cys was studied by fluorescence spectrometry. Fig. S4a shows fluorescent spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl after reaction with 20 μM Cys for different time. As shown in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence intensity at 555 nm increases significantly from 0 to 15 min, and the fluorescence intensity of the solution no longer increases after 15 min. The reaction adsorption kinetics was fitted well by the quasi-first-order kinetic equation, ln (I max-I t)/Imax = -Kt + C, where I max is the maximum fluorescence intensity of the solution at 555 nm, I t is the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 555 nm at different reaction time, K is the rate constant (min−1), and C is a constant. The linear regression equation is ln (I max-I t)/Imax = −0.129t-0.0180 (R 2 = 0.9902), so the rate constant is 0.129 min−1. The reaction between NBD-Cl and Cys can be completed within 15 min, so the optimal reaction time is 15 min for colorimetric and fluorescent analysis of Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of GLCNS Cys@liposome nanocapsules
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules were used as labels for colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay of S. aureus. The Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules were composed of dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine, dipalmitoyl phosphocholine, cholesterol, Cys, and anti-S. aureus antibody, so the fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to various components of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules were investigated in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3). As shown in Fig. S5a, NBD-Cl shows low fluorescent responses to other components of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules except Cys (2 mg L−1 for each). Because anti-S. aureus antibody and dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine contain amino groups, NBD-Cl shows obvious responses to these two substances at high concentrations (Fig. S5b). However, at the same concentrations, the fluorescent response to Cys is much higher than those to anti-S. aureus antibody and dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine (Fig. S5b). Because both anti-S. aureus antibody and dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine are macromolecules, the content of amino groups in anti-S. aureus antibody and dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine is relatively low. Thus it is reasonable that the response of NBD-Cl to Cys is much higher than those to anti-S. aureus antibody and dipalmitoyl phosphoethanolamine.

Cys@liposome nanocapsules were prepared similarly to that of small molecule-encapsulated liposome nanocapsules reported previously [20,21]. The morphology and size of Cys@liposome nanocapsules were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 a, b, Cys@liposome nanocapsules show spherical structure. The sizes of Cys@liposome nanocapsules range from 70 to 100 nm. Cys@liposome nanocapsules were further characterized by FTIR spectrometry (Fig. 3c). In the FTIR spectrum of Cys, the peaks at 3182 and 2978 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric and symmetric –CH stretching vibration of the CH2 group [22]. A weak band at 2554 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of –SH [22]. The peak at 2080 cm−1 is related to the protonated –NH2 [22]. The peaks at 1620 and 1397 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO- [22]. The peak at 1538 cm−1 corresponds to N–H bending vibration [22]. The peaks at 1295 and 1059 cm−1 are associated with the C–O and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively [22]. In the FTIR spectrum of liposome, the peaks at 2927 and 2850 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibration CH2 stretching vibration. The band at 1737 cm−1 is related to the symmetrical stretching vibration of CO [23]. The peak at 1270 cm−1 is related to stretching band of PO4 [23]. The Cys@liposome nanocapsules show the characteristic peaks of both Cys and liposome, confirming that Cys was successfully encapsulated in the liposome nanocapsules. To measure the amount of Cys in the liposome nanocapsules, the Cys@liposome nanocapsules were destructed by Tween-20 [21,24], and the released Cys molecules was measured by fluorescence spectrometry using NBD-Cl as the probe. The results indicated that the optimal destruction time was 30 min (Fig. S6a). TEM image in Fig. S6b also indicates that the liposome nanocapsules were completely destructed after being incubated with Tween-20 for 30 min. Fig. 3d shows fluorescence spectra of 20 μM NBD-Cl after reaction with the initial Cys@liposome nanocapsules and the destructed Cys@liposome nanocapsules in PBS-CH3CN solution (v/v = 7:3). As shown in Fig. 3d, NBD-Cl shows no obvious response to the undestructed Cys@liposome nanocapsules, but shows high fluorescent response to the destructed Cys@liposome nanocapsules. The result indicates that fluorescent response mainly results from the reaction between NBD-Cl and Cys. Based on the fluorescent response, the weight percentage of Cys in Cys@liposome nanocapsules can be roughly estimated to be 57.1%, implying that a great number of Cys molecules are encapsulated in each liposome nanocapsule.

To specifically recognize S. aureus cells, Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules were prepared by modification of anti-S. aureus antibody on the surfaces of Cys@liposome nanocapsules. To enable efficient magnetic separation, immunomagnetic nanoparticles were also prepared following our strategy reported previously [11]. To confirm the successful preparation of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules and immunomagnetic nanoparticles, SDS-PAGE was carried out. As shown in Fig. S7, a band is observed at ca. 48 kDa for the anti-S. aureus antibody alone (lane 1). Note that here Cys@liposome nanocapsules and magnetic nanoparticles without modification of anti-S. aureus antibody show no bands (data not shown). Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules (lane 2) and immunomagnetic nanoparticles (lane 3) show bands at ca. 60 and 72 kDa, respectively. Obvious retarded electrophoretic mobility is observed for Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules and immunomagnetic nanoparticles, confirming the successful preparation of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules and immunomagnetic nanoparticles.

The binding of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules with S. aureus cells was studied by SEM. The SEM image in Fig. S8 indicates that immuno-nanocapsules can be labeled to S. aureus cell. Fig. S9 shows SEM image of S. aureus cells separated from the solution using immunomagnetic nanoparticles, and the result indicates that immunomagnetic separation of S. aureus cells is feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay of S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay of S. aureus was carried out. The detection procedures are illustrated in Fig. 1. S. aureus cells were initially incubated simultaneously with Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules and immunomagnetic nanoparticles in PBS. The weight ratio of Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules and immunomagnetic nanoparticles was optimized to be 5:2 (Fig. S10a), and the incubation time was optimized to be 60 min (Fig. S10b). After mangnetic separation, Cys@liposome immuno-nanocapsules linked to bacterial cells were destructed by Tween-20, and then Cys molecules were released from the nanocapsules. Finally, the released Cys molecules reacted with NBD-Cl to generate colorimetric and fluorescent signals.

Fig. 4 a–c shows typical Photographs, UV–vis absorption spectra, and standard curve for the detection of different concentrations of S. aureus cells. The color of the solutions gradually deepens with the increase of S. aureus concentration (Fig. 4a). Especially, the chromogenic signals at the S. aureus concentrations from 40 to 1000 CFU mL−1 can be distinguished by naked eyes. As shown in Fig. 4b, the absorption peaks at 475 nm increase with the increase of the concentrations of S. aureus cells. The absorbance at 475 nm is proportional to the concentrations of S. aureus cells (Fig. 4c). The linear range is from 40 to 4000 CFU mL−1 (R 2 = 0.9985). As shown in Inset of Fig. 4c, when the S. aureus concentration is as low as 10 CFU mL−1, one can see obvious absorption peak. The detection limit (LOD) of the proposed method (10 CFU mL−1) is much lower than those of the previously reported colorimetric methods (Table S1) [4,25,26].

Fig. 4d shows fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 475 nm for the detection of different concentrations of S. aureus cells. As shown in Inset of Fig. 4e, when the S. aureus concentration is as low as 1 CFU mL−1, one can see obvious fluorescent signal. The fluorescence peaks at 572 nm in Fig. 4d increase with the increase of the concentrations of S. aureus cells. As shown in Fig. 4e, the linear range is from 4 to 4000 CFU mL−1 (R 2 = 0.9973). As listed in Table S1, the detection limit of our immunoassay (1 CFU mL−1) is much lower than those of the previously reported fluorescent methods [27–30]. The ultralow detection limit of our immunoassay can be explained as follows. (1) Our recent work has proved that immunomagnetic nanoparticles can enable nearly 100% capture efficiency for bacterial cells [31]. (2) A great number of Cys molecules are encapsulated in each liposome nanocapsule. (3) NBD-Cl can show high colorimetric and fluorescent responses to Cys.

In summary, by using Cys@ liposome immuno-nanocapsules as labels, a sensitive method for colorimetric and fluorescent immunoassay of Staphylococcus aureus was developed, based on colorimetric and fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to Cys. Due to the combination effect of liposome nanocapsule-amplification, high capture efficiency of immunomagnetic nanoparticles, and high colorimetric and fluorescent responses of NBD-Cl to Cys, the detection limits of the proposed method was as low as 10 and 1 CFU mL−1 for colorimetric and fluorescent detection, respectively. This work can not only greatly extend the application field of the NBD-Cl based reaction, but also provides a new design pattern for developing rapid, sensitive, and selective pathogen detection methods.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305041, 21705045 and 21775041), the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Hunan Province (2018RS3062), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2018JJ2252), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (17A125), and the Science and Technology Project of Changsha (KQ1802036). W. Deng and C. Cheng contributed equally to this work.

J. Clin. Microbiol.
